Hey everybody, welcome. We have a very special day today because my buddy David Deutz is on the call with us. David really needs no introduction, but I will introduce him anyway because you know that's what we do here for everywhere else when you have a guest you introduce your guest. So David is one of the greatest copywriters to ever walk the earth. He's had like multiple controls from multiple clients generating God knows how many hundreds of millions or more dollars in sales. Great record of beating the control, but the other thing about him, oh one thing I admire about David is that he is a perpetual student. He never stops learning, he doesn't think he's too fucking cool for the room. He always seeks out criticism of his stuff, he doesn't take it personally. He's always looking to get even better than he is and that's something that's true with all the great people, all the greats in any field. They're lifelong learners, they never rest on their laurels, they keep striving, but the other thing about David that I think is the most important thing as far as what we're going to be talking about today is that he is one of the most creative people I've ever met. Just used to look at some of the stuff and say, oh fuck am I allowed to swear on this? I just swore. I'm thinking his stuff and I'm like, I wish I would have thought of that. And I just, it used to be, I never considered myself a super creative guy, but as I would look at David and I'd be like, wow how do you do this? And he said, you know what? It's not just, I wasn't just, didn't just come out of the womb being created, creative, I use a system. And I'm like, huh, do tell. And that's when he turned me on to the system. It's just thinking inside boxes rather than thinking outside the box and it's unlimited all the possibilities. The biggest problem to creativity is when you have unlimited when so adding constraints actually makes you more creative. We've talked about that in the past on these calls kids. So if you ask me to write a limerick, that's freaking easy, which has to fit a certain meter, the first line rhymes with the second, the third and fourth rhyme, and then the fourth run rhymes with the first and second. And especially if you want to write a funny limerick, that's pretty easy. Give me an opening line and I can write a funny limerick because all I'll do is start looking for rhymes for that first sentence and one of them will be funny. And then now that you know what your first sentence in your last sentence is, then ones in between, they just the bridge to get you there. Right. And we all did that. Everybody in this room wrote funny limericks. Right. So the same thing with songwriting. You asked me to write a song. Okay, it's got meter. It's got a rhyme and it's got to have a tune. But you asked me to write free verse. I'm freaking lost. I have no idea where to begin. It's too broad. Right. So very often we find ourselves with a new project, a new copywriting project, and we're like, Oh my God, where do I begin? And that's where our process comes in the process that we've been learning about researching the audience, researching the product, putting notes together, categorizing them into different things, and then doing different ideas, different techniques to come up with ideas. And that's what we're going to be talking about today with our good friend, David. So a warm welcome for David Deutch. Thank you, Paris. That was great introduction. Why? You're welcome, David. So the peeps here got some questions. And most of the questions were about, can you give us examples of how you use the different boxes to come up with headline or theme ideas for ads? Because that's, we're all copywriters on this call. So that was the number one thing. So if you guys notice, just so far in the short time we've been talking together, David just naturally does these things that creative people do. He relates things to other things, what they're similar to, right? He did it like three times just now. He used, he's used metaphors already, right? He's done all that kind of stuff. So let's talk about it. This idea of taking something else and using that same thing and moving over to another application, which of the boxes is that? One of the things I was reviewing the boxes in preparation for this, and one of the things I noticed is to some extent there's over, that's not overlap, but you can call something different things, right? Like in a way, that's combining things, right? That's the combining box. How do I take this? How do I take this? How do I take celebrity and combine it with weight loss? And I know the celebrity weight loss program. Right. And that this is a similar idea. And that's true of, I know a lot of people are interested in how do I do this for copy? It's the same thing with actual copy, right? It's like, how do I write a headline that combines two interesting things together? Yeah. That's one of my favorite techniques is the combining things. You combine a Sony Walkman and a jukebox and you get an iPod, right? Yeah. It's what did Steve Jobs call it? Ideas having babies. Right? I love the example of the snowmobile, which is a combination of the handlebars of a bicycle, the treads from a tank and the skis from skiing and a motor in the back. And you put all that stuff together and you've got a snowmobile that can go over snow. Yeah. Yeah. So you had another great point. You said these boxes are not necessarily discrete, separate, mutually exclusive things. It could be more than one at the same time. And I find that's a, that's very often the case with great ideas. If you look at them and try to say, oh, what's the person doing here? They're doing multiple things. So I'm a big fan of stacking. I've talked in the past about things like proof stacking. So there is, there is stacking the techniques in this book and maybe you take a good idea and turn it into a great idea. So what was the next question? Yes. Yes. So question number two, talking a little bit about your methodology, David, as you use this to write controls, but specifically, do you find yourself cycling through the boxes before working on a new promotion? Or are they more or less ingrained in your mind so you don't even look at the list? I think to some extent they're ingrained, but if I'm stuck or I just want to be generative and generate a lot of ideas, sometimes it really helps to just go through it. Oh, how can I do the opposite? Or why you should never, you know, invest in the stock market? Why, how could I combine something? How could I take one little piece of this and isolate it? And a lot of times that'll get me to a couple more headlines than I might have ordinarily, or a couple of more ideas than I would have. Yeah. Like a lot of us, we sometimes things just come to us because it is ingrained and it is a part of the muscle memory. And sometimes you just got to grind it out. I think to some extent what this is like NLP. Yeah. They just, they looked at Erickson, they looked at Virginia Satir, they looked at all these people and they said, oh, look what they do, they do this, let's model that and extract techniques from it. It's the same thing. How to creative people's brains work. They seem to a lot of times they'll just flip something and do the opposite. A lot of times they'll combine things. So it's a little bit like that too. Yeah. And we're going to talk more about the various techniques and examples of them in a moment, which I think I'm really looking forward to. I think it's going to be amazing. I'm curious about this. What do you think David is the worst mistake copywriters make when looking for ideas for their ads? I think the worst mistake the copywriters make is ideas for the sake of ideas. Right. That a lot in financial, for example, right, where it's, I know we'll talk about the coming war with China or we'll talk about this. We'll talk about, there's this new cool discovery and we'll highlight that, right? But they don't think, and this is one thing I know Paris is really good at. So I assume you guys are as well, is really, okay, where are people's heads now? Right? They just lost the money in the stock market. So maybe they're in a, I got to make my money back. Right. I want to write something that is going to appeal to that desire to recoup loss. Okay. And then my ideas now can come from that. They can spring from that rather than just what I call ideas for just the sake of ideas, because it's clever or it's a cute reversal or something like that. So I think it's really important to ground ideas in the psychology of the market. So ideas that enter the conversation in the reader's mind or promise something that they're actually interested in. Right. Promise something they're interested in, but also take into account where they are psychologically. And that's stages of the market awareness and sophistication. That's what just happened in the stock market. It's been down. It's been flat, whatever it's been, the whole COVID thing, what happened with that? How are people feeling these days? Because that keeps changing and keeps evolving. And yeah, a lot of times when you see ideas, especially if you've been around a while like Paris and I have, you go, that's like an idea from 10 years ago. Right. That would have worked 10 years ago. But every day the market moved a little until it's not there anymore. Yeah. Yeah. So it was, I don't know who it was, if it was Joe Schrieffer, it was somebody at Agora who had the Venn diagram. The one circle is what people want. The other circle is what your product does. And the third circle is what's in the, I can't ever print. I don't know, Zeke, Zeke Geist, how do you, Zeke Geist, what's going on in the world today, whether it's current events or attitudes and stuff. And where are those three things intersect? That's where you want to be. So great. A lot of people miss that and they do something just for the sake of doing it. Now, I will add that I don't think there's anything wrong with coming up with ideas for ideas sake, because sometimes that may morph into something else. So it might be a good, absolutely. Exploraming exercise. What I thought you were going to say, which you didn't, but what I'm going to say, I think the biggest mistake is they don't generate enough ideas. They say, oh, that's very true. Here's an idea. I'm going to run with it. Right. Richard Armstrong, who's one of the great copywriters, he's been many years being what I would say was a journeyman writer and he'll stay the same thing. Right. He was good, but now he's one of the fucking best ever. Motherfuckers taking controls away from me. And he's, I heard him say on an interview, the difference between me now and me then was before I would get an idea. I go, oh, cool. I got an idea. Let me write about it. And now he says, oh, I got an idea. That's possibility. Let me see if I could come up with a better one. Yeah. That was one of the things I appreciated most about the course, David, was how it gave some ideas or gave some instruction on how to not self-censor and just come up with a multitude of ideas because that quality will emerge from the quantity. Yeah. That was one of the things I really appreciated about the course materials. Self-centering is a great way to say it too. That's the biggest thing. Yeah. Because I have my own way of coming up with ideas, not my own way, but I tend to have certain ways of thinking and certain ideas. And Paris has certain ways of thinking and certain ideas. So if we have something that enables us to get outside that, then that's very helpful because then I can come up with the kind of ideas maybe that Paris would. He can come up with the kind of ideas that I would because there's a system to draw that out of us where it's not natural. Cool. It's a parable. I enjoyed it because it reminded me of things like for me, it reminded me of the more powerful books that I've ever read and it had me glued to the page. So The Richest Man in Babylon, The Alchemist and things like The Way of the Peaceful Warrior and those kind of things that have all got little snippets of the lessons. But you just read it. Whereas I think I just wanted to know why you chose that pathway to get this together. Yeah, good question. I think like you, I was kind of inspired by Richest Man in Babylon and books like that, that were very powerful in that way of conveying something without here is what you should know. Here is a creativity technique that you should try. Who wants to read that? But everyone loves stories. So everyone does. Pauline, you're focused on a bunch of stories. It is. Yeah. Yeah. That's why I really admired it. I love David's writing anyway, like a bit of a copy crush. I've got two of my copy crushes here, Paris and David, of course. Next, I only have the main question like how did David come up with headlines and ideas using those boxes? Do you want to speak a little bit about it now or should we just save it for when we're giving going box by box and coming up with examples? Yeah, I can speak a little bit about it now, which the short answer is you just write something, right? And then you look at it, right? The herb that the herb that relieves arthritis pain, right? And then you look at it, you go, okay, how can I add something to this? Oh, the herb that relieves arthritis pain while you sleep, the herb from the Amazon that relieves arthritis pain. How could I, how could I do the opposite? How to increase your arthritis pain, right? Or how what you're doing and then that might lead me to why what you're doing now that you think is good for you is making your arthritis pain worse. That's a huge thing. When we talk about knowing the psychology of people and where they're at and stuff, right? Those of you who've seen my student talk, people walking around feeling defective, feeling like they're making mistakes, feeling like there's stuff they don't know about that they should feeling there's a big party going on out there and they weren't invited. And all this stuff feeds into it, like what David was saying before understanding their psychology. And it's not just an idea in a vacuum. It's an idea that has to do with what they want and what their psychology is. I'm a big fan of matrixes, right? What's his name? Uses Dan Sullivan, right? You this down this column, this up at the top. There's no better tool in the world than the four square matrix. So you could take all these techniques divided up, do the opposite, do whatever add, subtract, multiply down this column, then across the top, you could have whatever you want. You could have the schemas. Okay, how could I do these things to this in relation to people feeling inadequate? How can I do these things to this in relation to people wanting to be heroes? How can I do like you could even just simplify it and make it fear greed. Right, you could do the Bob Hacker stuff, greed, fear, anger, love, sloth, envy. You could do the schemas thing at not isolation, exclusion, effectiveness. What's the one where you think disasters are on the corner of the name? Whatever. Yes, and here we go. Catastrophic. No, I love that idea, David. That's fantastic. Yeah. Is that help, Shin? That we'll do some, like Paris said, we'll do some actual fun stuff with headlines. Yeah, that's great. Yeah. Okay, sounds great. Who was next with a question? Yeah, one question, which is you wrote at one point, the problem with the way most people go about solving problems is that they aren't clear what the problem really is that they're trying to solve. So I wondered what questions or methods do you use to clearly define the problem you're working on, the time to solve? You have to look at it a little bit on what the hierarchy of abstraction is. No, you guys, perfect abstraction is just you start with, I'm trying to think of something uncontroversial, but you start with houses, right, dwellings, right. And then the next level of abstraction is houses. The next level of abstraction is large, free standing houses. The next level of abstraction is $50 million mansions. The next level of abstraction is this $50 million mansion in Los Angeles overlooking the ocean. So just knowing where you are on levels of abstraction sometimes is really helpful. And it's the same thing with problem solving. I need more customers. That's the certain level of abstraction, right? It's a very high level of abstraction in a way. I need more customers. So you could go lower and you could go, well, why do you need more customers? I need more, I want more profits. As you go down, so the death, each higher level is more specific because you can go up and down. You can go either way to look at the problem, right? But if you're moving downward, maybe you got too specific, maybe getting customers is too specific because getting customers is only one way to get more profit. What do you care if you get more customers or not? If you're getting more profits, better have less customers. It is because you get, you, the profitability is in selling to your existing customers. But interestingly enough, the uninitiated, they think more customers is the answer. I remember when back in 1996 when Phillips did that newsletter with J. Abraham on business breakthroughs, and it was tailored to small business owners, which is a shitty market because there's a reason they're small business owners. But people were writing all these packages with all these different claims, like double your money in 18 months. Now you and I know that's easy as pie to do, but to the small business owner, that's not a believable claim, right? And so my headline was simply, and it was the best performing one. They ended up folding the product anyway, but my headline was more customers, fewer hassles. So that's, they fucking wanted. And then I had little examples of each. And this was a time when the big box stores were coming in, like the home depots and the bed bath and beyonds and all that. And the mom and pops were being put out of business. So a lot of the thrust of what we were using examples of this, like blockbuster moved in across the street from this guy, yet his sales doubled. How see page 23 inside and there was no examples like that. But it goes back to what you were saying of knowing what the audience wants, knowing their psychology, and knowing what's going on at a given time and place, because that was the perfect message at that time, but it wouldn't resonate at all today. We're a little bit mixing up two things here, just to separate them out a little bit. One is what customers want, which is more customers, right? And the other is what they don't know what they want, but they think they want more customers, which is more profits. Because to them, more customers equals more profit. Right. They're uninitiated. So what I did in the copy is I started with revealing ways to make more customers and then segue it into, you know what, that's not the best way to make money. The way to make money is to sell more to your existing customers, to get them to buy more often and so on and so forth. And you can learn more about it in your free report, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, that whole song and dance. But that is important, right? To have a very clear definition and sometimes to really look at that and experiment with it. I love that thing from Rory Sutherland, right? Where they came to him and they said, Hey, we want you to do a campaign. We're going to tear up all the track on the London to Manchester run. We're going to put in high speed track and it's going to be 10 minutes faster. And we're going to do a huge campaign to promote how much faster it's going to be. And he said, screw that, screw the advertising campaign, screw tearing up the track, just serve free drinks and give them high speed Wi-Fi. No one care how long it takes. Free drinks served by beautiful models. Yes. So they were solving the make the train faster by spending tens of billions of dollars. And he was the solving the dissatisfaction by spending exactly a hundred grand, maybe that if you have beautiful models serving you drinks and you don't give a shit that it's going to take longer, longer is better. Yeah. So yeah, again, comes back to what you were saying, David, about defining the right problem. It's a dad Kennedy said after 9 11 when they wouldn't give you utensils on the plane because they think it's a utensil problem. That's funny, but that's so true. That's such a great example. And when you talk about government and big bureaucratic organizations, they're notorious for solving the wrong problem. What do we call that? It's not a conversion problem. It's a leads problem. They're like, how can I get higher conversion? How can I get higher conversion? How would that leave conversion alone, get more leads? And often it's the opposite. Most people think it's a leads problem. They think I don't get enough leads. No, you suck at converting them or they think I'm not getting enough traffic. If you had a higher performing VSL, that problem would be solved because number one, you'd be converting the traffic you're getting. Number two, by having a kayak conversion rate, it allows you to spend more money on leads and on the traffic and get more leads. So yeah, people are often like, oh, I got to get more customers. Maybe you just got to sell more to your existing customers. So yeah, great stuff. I want to just circle back briefly on one of the boxes where you illustrated with arthritis to do the opposite. My thought would be like, why would you want to increase someone's arthritis pain? So my instinct would be to just ignore it doesn't apply to me. But then you showed how if you stick with that, oh, good. And this great idea can blossom out of that. Like how the solution you're using now is actually making your arthritis pain worse. So I think it illustrates the importance of just giving each of these a try, even if you think it's not going to apply to you or your situation, just playing around with it without any kind of expectations of what's going to happen. Just fiddle around with it, take it easy with it, and then see what can come from that. Yeah, a lot of creativity is just unblocking. Right. And a lot of people are blocked by, oh, this won't work. This won't work. This idea won't work. Whereas if you're not blocked by that, this idea, this idea, that list will stimulate some great idea. How do I make this work? I think we just identified the third big mistake people make. So we got ideas for the sake of ideas. We got not doing enough ideas. We got saying, oh, that won't work for me. How many times have you heard that, David? There's a couple of things in that. One is it connects to what we're talking about, which is the creativity part. But the other is it connects to the Richard Armstrong part, which is keep making things better. Us copywriters, the key is iteration. The key is polishing. Jim Rutts said, because I know Paris, how you like these words, Jim's words said, my approach to copywriting is very lapidary. He was like a jeweler, a lapidarist, right? He was polishing diamonds. He was chipping away. He was making it reflect light just the right way. And that's what we do, right? We take not a lump of coal exactly, but sometimes like with the headliner or the bullet, it's a lump of coal. And we go, yeah, if we shave this off, if we compress it with 100 million pounds of pressure, if we, what do they call it when you chip away at a diamond? I can't remember. Yeah, that's it. Fascinating. Yeah, you're fascinating. You're cutting. And then you polish it. Then you put in really nice settings. So it really looks good. That's what we do. Yeah. Can you come up with any examples from your own life where you had something that was decent and you kept improving it and it became something great? No, I'm putting you on the spot here. Yeah, I don't really, pretty much everything I've ever done started. Some things come fully formed. Only before I came up with beat any disease by taking advantage of its hidden weakness, there was a lot of stuff. This guy is great. Tomorrow's cheers today. How to get rid of all your pain and all your suffering. And it's yeah, okay. I kept trying to polish those. Sometimes it's just one word too. Sometimes it's just one unexpected, Paris and I love that headline from Eugene's reports. Man has sexual congress five times, 60 year old man has sexual congress five times a day. Just that Congress, just that incongruous use of Congress made that headline. Yeah. Yeah. Finding the right word can often make the big difference. And by the way, when I first read that headline, I thought 60 years old was old. How I look at it, I think 60 is young. 60, that's nothing. Which is of course an important lesson in how our markets look at things. If we're writing to people older than us, we've got to realize they look at things very differently. I remember one time I wrote a headline way back when I was a youngster for people over 50. And Michael Fishman looked at it and he said, they're going to look at that and they think you're writing to 50 year olds and they're 70 and you've lost them. I said over 50 doesn't matter. They're going to look at 50. They're going to say, oh, this doesn't apply to me. It's a great point. Yeah. It's really easy to disqualify the people that you're trying to market to. We don't realize how many things like that people go, okay, because they're looking to disqualify themselves, right? Because they don't really want to buy. They don't really want to keep reading this. They want to get back to binging on Netflix or whatever it is that they love to do. That's a great point. Their default setting is I want to delete this. Yeah. I've got too many damn emails. I want to go through them and delete as many as possible. Same thing with everything else. Now the other force is, and we were talking about this last week, people are bored out of their skull and people are procrastinating from doing work. So you've got that working in your favor. You make it entertainment, right? It's got to be edutainment. Would you agree? Yeah. Ogilvy, I think, said you can't bore people into buying. Yeah. And it's so true. If you're boring them, they're not going to stick around. Some will, and you'll make some stales, but you're losing an awful lot of people. Like current movies make this the most exciting thing that happens to them today. Yeah. Yeah. So great stuff. In many cases, the boxes that seem less, the least relevant that spark the best ideas. And I was just going to ask what kind of tricks you have or what do you do if you really get stuck and it's like, how do I can't come up with something? The writer's block version of this and the idea block. I think to some extent you just keep going because sometimes you come up with really great ideas for things up front, right? And sometimes you get to the end of the promotion and you go, oh, now I know what the headline should be. Or it pops up in the middle of the copy where you're writing something. Whoa, that's the headline I just wrote here. Yeah. Headlines are funny things. How they just come from nowhere sometimes. You're over here a conversation in a restaurant. Oh my God, that's a headline. So we train ourselves to have our antennas out for things. Like ideas come from strange places, right? Where just do it came from? Where? Was Gary Gilmore's final words before he was executed by firing squad? Because he was a serial killer. He said, just and he said, and he said, let's do it. Right. And Dan Wyden, the Wyden and Kennedy remembered that when he was trying to come up with a slogan for Nike and it became just do it. Look at that. I love stories about how things came about, right? Yeah. Ideas. I love anything that has to do with it. I've watched interviews with Stephen King telling about where the genesis of his projects, right? And I love reading anything about the stories behind how songs were written. Right. The Beatles, there's a bunch of books on how they did their songs, and we dove deep into that. There's also a channel on YouTube school of, I know, I'm sorry, Professor of Rock. And it's all stories about how the songs came about. And it's all the techniques that you're going to talk about in a moment. It's combining things. It's doing the opposite. Like a great example of doing the opposite is the talking head song, Nothing but Flowers. I don't know if the folks on the listening in Radio Land are familiar with it. There was the song, the famous song, Joni Mitchell wrote in the 60s. They took all the trees, put them in a tree museum. They tore down paradise and turned it into a parking lot. Right. Oh, they paint paradise. Paradise. Anyway, so that's like a, oh, that's pretty cool sentiment, right? David Byrne comes along with Talking Heads and he writes that where there was a 7-Eleven, now there's a peaceful oasis. He's lamenting the fact that there's nothing but flowers. They took away all my conveniences and turned it into, did the fucking opposite. Right. So there's doing the opposite. There's combining. A lot of times songs are combining very often literally. Like Billy Joel had three fragments sitting around in his unfinished songs and one day he's at a restaurant in the waiter goes, bottle of red, bottle of white. There's my opening and then he combined the three songs and he made it seems from an Italian restaurant. Beatles did it all the time. Paul would come in with a fragment. Jon would come in with a fragment. They'd just make it a song. I love the, I love the, I forget what they call those things for movies. Like the basis of movies, the little tagline. Yes. And like Star Wars is just classic western plus out of space, outer space. Put them together, you get Star Wars. The Flintstones, classically, was the honeymooners plus the stone age equals the Flintstones. Plus a cartoon. Plus a cartoon. You could combine more than two things. Yeah. And then when you got something, you could combine more things. So a moment ago, we talked about Walkman plus jukebox equals iPod, iPod plus phone plus Palm pilot equals iPhone. Right. And then they just kept adding more stuff to it. Right. So again, you in your course about taking things away and adding things in the beginning, the iPhone was all about taking things away. No, all the other MP3 players had too much crap on it. That was designed by engineers for engineers. We're designing it for a consumer. We're having this plane thing and it's going to have just a wheel on it. Remember the original line? Yeah. And now of course you look at the iPhone and it's the exact opposite because now they went from taking things away to adding things, right? To where the point where you got like all this crap on here, right? All these apps and shit. So yeah, everything's like everything else. Creativity is creativity and is creativity. Let's get into the nitty gritty. Let's go through the boxes one at a time and talk about them. Okay. Why don't you do the honors service? You're the one who created this masterwork. Welcome back boys and girls. We are here with the great David Deutsch talking about creativity. David has a course called idea power, which is one of the best creativity courses I've ever seen. If you don't think you're creative, if you think you're too analytical or whatever, well then this course is perfect for you because it gives you a paid by numbers thing. You just go through these different boxes and use them to generate tons and tons of ideas. And we're going to talk about them now. We're going to go through each box and come up with examples. So what's the first box, David? The first box is add new dimensions and that includes everything from the five senses is one of my favorites, like the Ted Nicholas ad, how to get a standing ovation every time you speak. He added that sight and sound of getting a standing ovation. It was even the photograph, a great photograph from the speaker's perspective of looking out to that. It reminded me of the disaster movie, the earthquake movie where they added sense around. Remember that? Yeah. We're dating ourselves with this. And then who was it? John Waters, one of those twisted people made their movie an odorama. It sounds like John Waters. Right. But yeah, but I'm glad you gave an actual example from advertising and direct response. In a sense, Bill Bonner is a control for international living that ran all those years. I don't know. Yeah, he brought you inside the garden. But that was the opening, right? You're, yeah, mixering it, you're smelling it, you're tasting it. Your gardener cuts a fruit from your private grove in your house. And he, yeah. So that's cool stuff. Anybody else want to chime in? Anybody else have any examples of adding other senses? I think Disney, Disney makes its whole business on that. Like you walk through Disneyland and there's the walk pass of bakeries. And although there's nothing there, you can smell it. And then Winnie the Winnie the Pooh ride in that. It's distinctly smells of honey. And then they actually use like different senses all the way through, like the whole Disney experience. What's another example, David, of, thank you, Pauline, for that example. What's another example, David, of adding another dimension? You talked about senses, but there were some others weren't there. I think as a headline trigger, right? It triggers you to bring in sensory experience into the words that you use, right? Imagine hearing like, what is it? They laughed when I sat down at the piano. But when I started to play, that's very sensory, right? That's very experiential. Imagine hearing people telling you, wow, you're so successful, right? Imagine seeing yourself on stage. Imagine feeling more confident than ever. All those things bring sensory elements into your headlines and just give it, just give a different dimension to the headlines. Add something to it. Make them more experiential. And imagine is one of the most powerful words you can use and copy. Remind me, Mr. Manbringer, were there two other subcategories within that box? Let's see. You can think of dimension, as with a lot of these boxes, you can think of them in different ways, right? You can make them, you can have a dimension of stillness versus movement, right? You can have a dimension of randomness versus order, right? So maybe, how do I, how is this about bringing order into the universe or into someone's life? How is this about not randomness makes me think, what about the whole, do you want to be subject to the winds of change all through your life? Be blown in any direction by the wind? Or do you want to take control and set your own sails and steer the rudder toward where you want to go in life? So there's a lot of different when you think about dimension, right? What are their dimensions of? More or less of this, right? Would you consider time a dimension? Yeah, absolutely. I think time is one of the most, and we can go off on a whole tangent about time, but just this idea of going back in time, taking someone ahead in time, right? And just being aware. The problem, defining the problem takes them from the past to the present, and then your solution takes them from the present to the future. One of the things I always find when people tell stories in copy is that they're not really giving you a good sense of where you are in time at any given moment. Were you talking about back here? Are you talking about now? And this has been verb tenses and making you confused. There's nothing more painful than watching somebody who can't tell a story. Tell a story. It's like an eight-year-old or something. I have a buddy I grew up with. It's really interesting because he's really funny and bigger than life, but for some reason just can't take a tell a story, and we're like, I'm really trying hard. It story has to have a point to it, number one. There has to be a chronology to it. Even an arc of... Yes, yes. Now we're getting it to advanced. Now again. Story arc. Yes. And there has to be someone who's out to get something and how he goes about getting the obstacles in the way. But that's pretty much it. And it's just like there's all this extraneous shit and tangents and all that stuff, and I don't know what I'm supposed to be getting from this. I'm not getting what the point is. What's that Pixar thing? And then, and something happened, and then. There's a great little Pixar. There's all these different formulas. There's the hero's journey. There's all that stuff. So anyway, we're going off on a tangent now. We are. I was thinking of how realtors, a lot of times they'll put like the scented candles. Yeah. I don't know whether it's like chocolate chip cookies or something like that to make it give it that real homey feel and make people want to stay a little longer and yeah. And to just associate good feelings with that house. I smell chocolate chip cookies baking. I remember my mother's chocolate chip cookies. I want to buy this house, right? Yeah. Yeah. I looked at a house once and it had like a setting outside on the deck with it was ready for dinner or lunch or something like that. There was little plates and things and I thought, oh, that's nice. I guess they were going to have dinner and they left and like, no, they set that up like that because that would give me a feeling of that's what you do with this house, right? You can eat outside and it's very nice and homey and warm. It's inviting and yeah. And inviting. Yeah. Yeah. I don't think I ever ate outside, but it's interesting because a few things come to mind here. Number one, I was a realtor in my previous career. So we used to do all that stuff staging and part of it was what you're talking about and there's a fireplace. You better fucking light a fire in that fireplace, right? The scented cancels or a little trick I used to do. I used to take vanilla extract and put drops on it on the light bulbs and it would smell the whole house would smell like vanilla. Getting rid of clutter, getting rid of personal photos because you want the person to project their life onto that house and picture living there and it's hard for them to do that if there's pictures of the other person's grandma and the other person's right. So you get rid of all that crap and you want to have a blank canvas in a way, right? On which they could project their old things. We call that remember when you and I were at Harlan Kilstein's course, NLP course, where we talked about being artfully vague. That is what that is. Your headline that we mentioned a moment ago beat any disease. Can you say it better because you probably know it verbatim? Yeah, how to beat any disease by taking advantage of its hidden weakness. That's artfully vague. Right. They're projecting onto it. Oh, if they have arthritis, they think you're talking about arthritis. If they think if they have cancer, they think you're talking about cancer and so on down the line. It's what Mel Merv Griffin used to call the Vanna White effect. When Vanna White was like the most popular person on the planet for that period of time, Merv Griffin said that's because they know nothing about her. So they're projecting all the positive qualities onto her and assuming that she has them. So there's times when you want to do that and you've done it many times with your headlines. So that's another thing to think about. That might fall under the broadening box. Right. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. So let's go to the next box. What would that be, sir? Doing the opposite. Of course, that's the endless examples of that. Right. And the best one to me is Arthur Johnson's had enough package for health where he was like had enough of people telling you not to drink coffee, not to do this, not to do that and to exercise. Exercise is really not that good for you in a certain way and coffee is great for you in a certain way and too much water you'll drown. And but it was just so the opposite. Right. P90X. Everyone's oh, this is a nice easy exercise program and you're going to love it because you barely have to break a sweat. They're like, no, you're going to break a sweat. Damn it. This is going to be the hardest exercise you've ever done. Do you have what it takes? And that's of course like the positioning of my copycub program for the fate of heart. Yeah. What were you going to say? Of course, what? Oh, of course, the classic, the case against just whatever it is. This is the case against it. Nice. Yeah. Chris Rock says doing the opposite is his number one technique, go to technique for coming up with material. Oh, that's interesting. So he takes any topic, he goes bullying. Okay, I'm going to do a whole thing where I argue that bullying is good. Does this all fit on it? Yeah. I'm glad you gave us a direct response and advertising examples for years. The one that always came to mind was Gary Benzavengas. Get rich slowly, right? Yeah. Yeah. But you came up with some other great ones now. Thank you for that. So I think let's go to the next one. Divide, right? How do you make something easier by dividing it up, right? Divide and conquer. How do you sometimes you have a headline and it's like that headline, right? How to beat any disease by taking advantage of its hidden weakness. You could divide that up how to beat arthritis by taking advantage of its hidden weakness. Yeah, it's a little different. It's the same thing as struck that applies to structuring copy. How do you divide up your argument into chunks? Hen big lies about diabetes. That's a nice way to divide up what you want to talk about. Yeah. Structure something. Yeah. So an example I like to use is when I would write for an alternative health newsletters like Health and Healing or Second Opinion or whatever, you would have 15 premiums, one for each ailment. And boardroom didn't really lend itself to that. And plus back then they were doing like we only do three premiums. Right. Before Eric Beto will convince them to offer 100. So what I would do is I would write my 700 bullets, then we would focus group the bullets to pick the best hundred that go in the promotion. Now that I have the 100, I would divide those into categories. I go, what are some of the commonalities? Oh, look, there's a whole bunch bullets here about how not to get ripped off. We'll make a premium out of it. Oh, look, there's a whole bunch of premiums about folk remedies and home remedies. We can make a premium of that. Oh, look, there's a whole bunch of premiums about like new cutting edge medical technologies. We'll make a premium about that. And so that's what I did with every newsletter promo I ever wrote for them. So yeah, dividing and categorizing. And people love categorizing. Perry Belcher talks about this. If you say, oh, my wife and I are expecting a baby, what's the first thing people ask you? Oh, your girl. Is it a boy or girl? They want to categorize. And that's why quiz funnels work. People want to put themselves in categories. Remember all that shit on Facebook? Which Harry Potter character are you? Buzzfeed quizzes. Yeah, Buzzfeed. Buzzfeed built an empire with that shit. And now they have all your data. I always thought one of the best. Sometimes these things are also strategy, right? Like marketing strategy. Yeah. And back in the day, in the 1920s or whatever, when people were buying cars and it was a lot of money to buy a car, General Motors came up with the idea to divide up the payment. There was a radical concept at the time to have installment payments. You didn't have to pay everything at once. So sometimes going through these things, just from a marketing strategy point of view, can give you incredible ideas. Installments are a huge game changer when you're selling anything high ticket. How do you divide up a product? I'm doing that now with some of my products, because people aren't so ready to pay $2,000 anymore or $1,000. So how can I take it up and divide it into a bunch of $49 products? And then the opposite is true as well, which we're going to get to in a little bit. Right. Amalgamate. Combine them. Amalgamate to make them bigger. Take the $49 products and add them together and make it a high ticket thing. So any other examples about dividing? Any examples for hooks or headlines? I think anytime, like we were talking about iterating, right? How do you take a headline and can you divide it up? Can you, that's the next, actually, the next box is take something out. Right. So it's very related. Right. Divide it up. And then you take it out like Ogilvy did at 60 miles an hour. The loudest noise in this new Rolls-Royce comes from the electric clock. Right. He really divided it up and then took out that one little thing, quiet, right? And the one little thing that exemplified the quiet, right? He didn't say the Rolls-Royce is quieter than an isolation tank or like a snow-filled meadow at dawn. He divided it up into just the niches thing he could and made that a little tiny analogy. Just hear the clock going. So taking things out. I just did that recently. We talked about the original iPad, Pod. It was all about taking things out, right? Simplify. Simplify. What's his name? Coach. The guy who wrote the 80-20 rule and started. Oh yeah, Richard Coach. Richard Coach. He's about, he wrote a book called Simplify. He said the dominators in their field, they're simplifying. They're either simplifying the price proposition, making it, finding a way to deliver something almost as good for a way lower price, like IKEA is the perfect example of that. They found that if they have the stuff in boxes and you'd cart it home yourself, now they don't need a showroom, they don't need trucks, they don't need any of that shit. And you buy furniture for a tiny fraction of the price and in return you agree to take it home yourself and assemble it. Then the other one is value proposition simplification. And that's what Apple's done. Just products that are an absolute delight to use and you can charge a premium price. I recently had an experience because for years people have been asking me, are you going to do a course or are you going to do a mastermind or are you going to do coaching? And I always said no because I'm like, I don't want to be in the group business. I don't want to be famous. I don't want to create a fucking huge mailing list and now you have this insatiable beast that you have to keep feeding it content all the time. I don't want to, there was a whole bunch of, I don't want us. And a mentor of mine said, you're just using that to stay in your comfort zone. That's bullshit. And I got really mad at him, started arguing with him and he just let me go on and he goes, listen man, you need to do any of those fucking things. Take all those things out. And he showed me how to do it without building a list, without being famous, without teaching my secrets to the masses. So now I'm doing like these little groups and I'm fucking having a blast doing them. So sometimes the starting point of taking something out, like if you want to use that box, is what don't I like? Other people like just take it out. Oh, you could do that. You could do that. Sometimes you're caught up in the, oh, I can't do that. Like you were saying a moment ago, oh, this doesn't apply to me. I can't do this thing. Yeah. A lot of marketing is just taking things out. Let's take out this segment only market to this segment, right? The people that have the highest potential. Right. The whole without thing, right? Take out the gluten, take out the sugar, take out this, take out that, take out what people don't like. Right. Yeah. And what people don't like is on things are universal, right? For, right. Ever. And other things are trends. Like, oh, I don't want, like back, they used to think X is bad for you. Now they think it's good for you and vice versa. Right. So people think, oh, milk's good for you. And now it's, no dairy free, right? Yeah. Sometimes we, we have multiple benefits in a headline. So instead of having multiple benefits, we take all of them out and just focus on one. Yeah. Yeah. Multiple benefits usually reduce response instead of increasing it. What about David Dandor Paris? Would it be an example of dividing when say you have a promotion that has a control and it starts to fatigue, but instead of writing a new headline, you just take a subhead from the promotion and cut that out and put that up as your headline, or maybe you're hired to revive a fatigue and control. Would that be an example of doing that, of dividing? Yeah, for sure. We're taking something out. You take the subhead out and put it up on top. Right. Yeah. So here's an example of taking something out. When our nutritional supplement promotions, back in the day, the common wisdom was you want to add a premium that they get free if they buy the vitamins. And it's something that's low cost to you, but high perceived value to them, right? Makes sense. So everybody was offering special reports that come with the promotion. And if you buy a bottle, you get this special report. If you buy three bottles, you get an additional special report. If you buy six bottles, you get another special report. And everybody in the industry was doing it. And we decided, what if we just took the fucking special reports out? They're pain in the ass. They cost us money. Are they really lifting response? Let's find out. We put it to a test. As soon as we took out the special reports, not only did response not go down, it went up. And so we go, wow, look at that. We did this promo and we took out the reports. Let's try it for another product. We took out the special reports. Response went up for that one. We tried it for a third product. We took out the special reports. Response went up for that one. And then we just stopped doing special reports. And I think that circles back to what David said earlier about being aware of what's going on, what's contemporaneous, maybe nowadays information, it's just not perceived as valuable as it was 10 or 15 years ago. Yeah, that was our theory as to why that happened was like, oh, before the internet, everybody wanted this. Now nobody wants this stuff. Now here's an interesting thing is, again, everything goes in cycles, right? And it's now at the point where people like getting a physical newsletter. So if you give them a downloaded thing, it just, they never open it. They just download it onto their hard drive, never look at it again. Whereas if you send them something in the mail, they look at it and it's a great customer bond to a lot of these internet marketers are sending hard copy newsletters to their customers now. Perry Marshall, for example, a bunch of other folks. And of course, people like Ben Settle, Dan Doberman, been doing that for a while. Dan Doberman. Oh, whatever. Doberman Dan. Doberman. But Dan Doberman, he's cool too. I like him too. Oh, gosh. All right, what's next? What's next? Bringing together, we talked about, right? How do you bring two ideas together? Celebrities and curapor arthritis. One of my favorites is when Joe Vitale needed a title for his book on PT Barnum. And I thought of the, there's a sucker born every minute. And I said, let's combine that with customer. There's a customer born every minute. Which was the perfect headline for that. Yeah, it was a great headline, great title. If you look at anything, right? eBay combines auctions and the internet. Where it combines, whatever it combines, taxis with communication and being able to just the tracking and all that stuff, which had just developed. But we're constantly doing that in headlines, right? How can we bring, what can we put together? Right? I've often just gone through a swipe file and taken the beginning of one headline template and the end of another headline template and put them together. So my headline that I wrote many years ago, the, what was it? Secret flaw in the financial markets lets you make back every dime you lost in the crash or whatever it was. I was literally just took two headlines from my swipe file and combined them. That's all that was. Of course, I pretended like I was this great genius who thought of this original thing. But that is always genius. Jackson Pollock combined splattering with painting or whatever it is. And it was genius and it was genius exactly how he did it. How about Andy Warhol with the soup cans? Yeah. But we're always trying to do that, of course, with pop culture in current events, because current events are already made, pop cultures are ready made source of attention getting. Yeah. So, you see that. Not all the heavy lifting for you. Now all you have to do is piggyback on something that's already in the consciousness of the reader or the viewer. Would you say like the documentary style VSLs? Yeah. Yeah, that's combining two things. Now the fake podcast can be the next evolution. Yeah. I think that was Alan Sultanich who came up with that one. So every great breakthrough, we talked about this on our call last week, in has come from new ways to hide the fact that you're selling something. So we went from direct mail with teaser copy on it. Obviously, you're being sold something. So it goes into the trash. And then Gary Hilbert says, what about making it look like a letter from your Aunt Millie? So the A-Pile envelope, right? Just live stamp, plain typewriter font or handwritten. That was a big breakthrough. Then the Magalogue, oh, a sales pitch disguises a magazine, then the bookalogue, oh, a sales pitch disguises a book. Then the VSLs, then the fake documentary VSLs, then the fake infomercials are just sales pitch disguises, the TV show. And now, of course, the sales disguises. I always wonder how much of it is really, we're fooling anyone, and how much of it is a kind of willing suspension of disbelief. I know this is a sales thing, but it looks interesting. So I'm going to give them the benefit of the doubt and read it, and they get sucked into it a little bit. And the answer is it's both. Yeah. Like when you and I see it, we're like suspending disbelief, but we get customer service calls still to this day, asking about that magazine we sent them in the mail, or that book we sent them in the mail. Agoura, when they did the plague of the black debt, they would get, of course, I want to buy copies of your book, one book, we don't even publish books back then. They found out when they were talking about the fucking direct mail piece, and they're like, oh, okay, yeah, we could sell those to you. And I think they started selling them for $10 a pop or something. And now they're actually, people are paying them to get the sales pitch. And by the way, that's a great example of doing the opposite. Instead of you paying to get your sales pitch out there, how people pay you to get, to hear your sales pitch. And that's also an example of taking something from one environment, moving it to your environment, taking it from the seminar environment, where you got the $50 seminar, it's really just a sales pitch. So I have a friend, she's in the real estate business, and she buys, she finds like, there's the big change, the coldwell bankers and all that stuff. And then there's the independence, and most of them are struggling. So my friend, Cherie goes and buys them. But guess what? People pay her to buy it from them. Pretty freaking crazy, huh? Anyway, what's the next box bring together? The other one is it is a bring it together, but the idea of one thing inside of an out like those Russian, I forget the name Mara Coppett, yeah, Mara Chushka, some Mara Chushka dolls, nesl inside each other. What can we put in a sales letter? Oh, look, we nestled in a little quiz, right? It just makes you think it triggers things, which is really all creativity is, right? It's not that someone is more creative than we are. It's that they know how to trigger ideas. So we just have to learn how to trigger ideas, right? It's a learnable skill. And that's very learnable skill. People don't get people think you're either creative or you're not. And anyone can be more creative. Anyone can come up with lots of ideas. So when you say putting something in something, I think of those fundraising letters where they give you a free pen or free address labels or free whatever. It's J. Abraham, I think was the first one that had the idea that people are sending out newsletters every month. Why not put an insert in there that sells something? Yeah. That was just a great idea. And of course, now it's common practice. It's so funny. Everything we do today, like that's common practice today, in some way, shape or form was either originated or popularized by Jay. They would say, just why don't you just mail your competitors offer to your list? What's that? That's insanity. Why would I do that? Now everybody does it. Now everybody does it. It's called affiliate marketing. Exactly. That's funny. I love that you talked about the international living letter. One of the things they did with that was actually to take that letter, envelope and all, and put it inside another envelope. I remember that. Yeah. A little note that says, hey, just in case you missed this. Yeah. Brilliant way to get more mileage. So put something inside something. Right. What can you put inside something? Could you put inside your promotion, inside your product, inside your fulfillment kit when they, the unboxing experience is very big now. The bar has been raised by Apple. Yeah. Good stuff, David. What's the next box? Next one. Do it beforehand. What can you do before you do something in order to increase the effectiveness? Could you send a postcard that says, hey, Magalog is coming. You should look for it. How can you predispose people? What can you remember that cabinet of the radio commercial? Look on page 27 of today's New York Times for our ad. Right. And they found that it lift your spots. And the original people who did it were the Columbia Record and Tape Club when they had the ads and TV guide. And how do we measure if the radio ads going to work or not? And the way they measured it was they had that little gold box. When you got the TV guide and their ad was in there, there was a little gold box and don't tell you what the box is. And the radio commercial would say, you'll find a little gold box that lets you add even an extra record for free. Oh, that's cool. And they knew how so many of the orders were doing that. And they knew that the radio ad was working. I think in a way, we're constantly trying to do things that beforehand, like we're constantly trying to immunize objections, right? We're trying to object, we're trying to address objections before they even articulate that. Just at the moment that it's coming into the person's mind, you're raising it, you're like a freaking mind reader. Yeah. So think about what can you do upfront or even before the thing goes out that can predispose people to look for the blue envelope, it's coming, look in your mailbox. The product launch formula did that in a way too, right? Jeff Walker thing, yeah. Yeah, it's going to say launches are all about doing it beforehand, but also dividing something up. Jeff Walker divided up the sales letter instead of it being like vertical, it was like horizontal. So splitting it up, but also doing stuff beforehand to get them excited about your launch. Yeah, yeah, yeah. That's, that's great. Pauline, that's a great example. That's exactly what those things are. So what about bullets or headlines where, where somebody reads it, that thing it's going to be, you're going to be talk about something and then you say, no, it's not about this or that. Is that part of what you're saying? Yeah, I guess that is in a way because they're going to think about, right? They're going to start to think like Paris always says, you don't want them thinking, oh, I know what this is, or this is just, you know, this, it's going to be a lot of work. It's going to be a lot of money. No, it's not that. It's not what you're thinking. It's not what you're even going to be thinking. You're going to address that beforehand so they don't stop reading because they think they know what it is. And it's not good. Awesome. Convert to a benefit. You all know that in a way. It's make lemonade out of lemons. And that's so powerful a lot of times in copy where you take something like, yes, it costs a lot. And here's why it costs a lot. This benefit, this feature, this, it's a very powerful way to, it's that pointing out the defect of something, right? Where someone points out, oh, look, on the bottom where you can't see it, there's a little scratch in this antique. If you bring it up, you get points for being upfront about it. And then you use it in a kind of judo to talk about why it has this, why it's so expensive, why it's so heavy, why it's so whatever, why the pill is so big, why, why it costs so much. The damage, the damage fruit mailings that Harry and David used to do. Oh yeah, classic. This Doreen, the taste bad, that's how you know it's working. Awesome. What's the next one? Switching gears. Just something, again, something to think about, not in a literal sense, but switching gears, like going from fast to slow. Copy has a pace, right? Copy headlines have a pace. They can be quick. You should get this or you ever read something like by Port of Stansbury, by Stansbury or by Mark Palmer. It's such a, what? J. Peterman. Yeah, it's got a slow pace, right? It's, let me tell you about how I see the world. But it's very interesting. But it's not this hyperbolic, going to make you so much money. It's much more calm. So just thinking about how can I speed this up? Or how can I slow it down? It's like a Coen Brothers movie also is slowed down the most of the stuff that's out there today. Sometimes you can literally speed up a VSL. You can literally make it. Almost every VSL we run, we look at them, we go, yeah, let's make it a little faster. And you have to be careful because if you make it too fast, the guy sounds like a chipmunk. Always, almost always, we end up speeding them up. Switching gears. And one thing I like about switching gears too, is going all the way, going up to 11 on the dial, really going overboard. Because when you start out overboard, it's a lot easier to throttle back and make it more believable, make it more compliant than it is to start out very careful about not going overboard, very careful about being compliant, and then saying, oh, I need to goose it up a little bit. Much harder to breathe life into a dead thing than to go to him down something that's over the top. And you said, something earlier that's related to this, when we were talking about this doesn't apply to me thing, started to, maybe you actually, what did I do here? I don't know if you fully articulated, but you started going in that direction. One of the best questions you can ask yourself is, how can I blank without blank? Instead of saying, oh, it's impossible because of blank, say, well, how can I do it without blank? And the same applies here to what you just said a moment ago. The same question can be posed to that box. Oh, see, revising, switching gear, revising the rhythm. When you think about rhythm, and you think about even a mailing, like I'm mailing every, or I'm mailing every other day, the copy has a certain rhythm, right? Bullet benefit proof, benefit proof. How can you mix that up? Should you put all your benefit together and then all your proof? Or should you mix up the rhythm of benefit proof? Just something to think about, right? Because in a way, everything is rhythm. There's a rhythm to life. There's a rhythm to copy. There's a rhythm to how getting a little esoteric, but there's a rhythm to how prospects respond to what you tell them. And if you can understand that and have a sense of it, and are able to have some control over it, then it becomes very different. Your writing takes a different quality to it, because you're aware of speed, speeding up, slowing down. It's like you read some copy sometimes by writers, and you get to the end, and you're like, there's something wrong here. What is it? I don't know what it is, because it's the same kind of copy. It's good. It's okay. And the problem is they haven't sped up toward the end. Yeah, that's what I always do is take the early copy blocks are bigger, and then they get progressively shorter as you go through the promo. So it gives you the illusion of speed and momentum, and they're just flying through, and by the end, they're just like ready to buy. And so many times people are like, oh, and that makes me think of another benefit that this wonderful product offers. It's a salesman who keeps talking after you already made the selling talks his way out of the sale. Yep. Do it itself. This is a very interesting concept where how do you make, and a lot of these things come from engineering, right? That's like, how do you make a machine self-regulate? How do you make a machine like tell you when it's time to change the filters? Right? How do you make a machine write copy for you? How do you make a machine write copy for you? So what can we get? What can we get people to do themselves, right? Like self-checkout is a way. How can we get our prospects to self-qualify, for instance? So when you first said do it yourself, I thought done for you, but you're saying the opposite too. Can apply. Yes. Right. What can we put the owner, because a lot of these things work both ways, right? Yeah. But you're right. How do we get? Like you said, you could go more general, more specific, higher, lower, everything, diminish, try and add all that stuff. It's all one of your boxes doing the opposite, but in a sense almost every tactic here is you could do the opposite. Yeah. Awesome. Yeah. What's the next box? This is probably just a little bit of a philosophical thing, but I think the problem with a lot of writing is that writers don't let prospects do certain things for themselves. They don't let prospects come to their own conclusions. They're like two plus two equals four rather than two plus two. You know what that equals, right? You don't have to tell. What would be an example of that? Just resisting that impulse to say, and that means you really need our product, right? It's no, just let them, if you have a cure for cancer, I always use that because so much, that's like the classic unbelievable kind of quack thing in Peter's box. Maybe rightfully to some extent. So if you have something that cures cancer, let's say you had a cure for cancer, right? You don't want to say, I've got a cure for cancer. Let me show you. It's great. It's not good. You want to say, hey, look at this. Look at this evidence. Look, they put this stuff in a picturgis with cancer cells and all the cancer cells died. And then they gave it to a mice that had cancer and the mice all got better. And it's got this in it. And this thing in it is proven to stop tumor cells from growing. So let the person themselves go, wow, this is like a cancer or something. You reminded me of something that Kevin Hogan wrote about, where he said there are studies that if you point out commonalities with people they like you, if you explicitly say, oh, we have a lot in common, they dislike you because they've been trying to manipulate them. It's interesting. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, I think you need to leave some room in copy for people to, to, as David said, to come to their own conclusions. You could sometimes can be too prescriptive. I see copywriters say, as you're sitting there tearing out your hair, facing your hands in despair and that sort of stuff. And so if they're not that, you're doing the emotions for them and it's not right. It's fake empathy copy. Exactly. But if you allow them to, if you build it enough of a case and like what David's talking about, that gradualization to give them the space then to come to their own conclusion, this is what women do to men all the time, by the way, we set up the scene and then gradualization, then you think that it's your idea and it's yes, dear, that's fantastic. But if we just leave that space in copy, I call that copy choreography, because I'm helping them to step through the copy that I want to, but I'm letting them take the steps. I'm not doing the steps for them. Like I'm not taking over and doing the performance. So that's something that I use when I'm writing just to the reader. That's great. There's a quote, it makes me think of a quote by Howard Gossage, the advertising guy. And he said, when baiting the mouse trap, be just be sure to leave room for the mouse. It's so brilliantly vague. It's like, what does that mean exactly? It makes sense, like after what you just said, leave room for the mouse. Because you're right, you got like we're talking about in the beginning, right, with benefits, people go, here's more benefit, more benefit, more benefit, more benefit. Yeah, but there's no room for it. It's just all these benefits, all the benefits that I don't believe. Okay, change the intensity. Right, this is so important with copy, right? Copy has a certain intensity to it. Right, it's intense. And to be aware of that and to use, of course, varying the rhythm, right? So it's intense for a while, it gets unintentional. Or maybe you've written some copy, and it's or a headline, right? And it's what if I really ramped up the intensity on this? What if I really put in all the adjectives and just got all John Carlton on it, right? And again, you could do the opposite. And you can do the opposite. John Carlton or you could go all Gary Benzvenga. Both work. You can wrap the intensity, you could tone down the intensity. There was a headline that it was why gold and why silver will rise. And the copyrighted turned down the intensity on it, said why gold, why silver may rise, and it worked better. It's such a copywriting lesson in that that just that believability and resistance. People resist the salesperson. People resist. But they love a trusted advisor. So it would be a trusted advisor. And if you can just lower resistance, then it's just you just have to go like that and make the sale. Talk about engineering in electrical engineering, there actually is a resistance. That's right. Yeah. Yeah. So whatever techniques they use to do that probably would work for whatever we do. So can I just add something about interaction? If I may? Yeah, please. So the way I explain this to my mentees and people that are coaches, when we go into the sale act for your court of action and your clothes, we know that we're going for the jugular. That's what that's the whole purpose of the copy and taking them on the journey. But then you've got the intensity reminds me of the fact that you can either go in and rip their throat out like a werewolf, or you can be more vampire where the person actually goes, yes, please, my blood, that kind of thing. Yes. I think with intensity, you can turn it up if you need to. But then also just know that you can turn it down and be that seductive, like someone is just willingly just saying, yes, take my money, please just do it. So anyway. Yeah. Yeah. You almost want them like, boy, I sure hope there'll be an opportunity to pay these people some money. Yeah. And you know what's funny is I've been doing a lot of that now with this new coaching thing. And it's so much easier to sell a high ticket thing than it is to sell like a $49 product. A $49 product, you need 10,000 words of copy. A high ticket product in the thousands of dollars is like a 10 minute phone call. And you just, I don't even close them. I do like Pauline said, like with the vampire, like, what's your problem? And they tell me their problem. And then I either say, oh, I think I can help you, or I don't think I can help you, or I can help you with this part, but not that part. Are you in our nunning? And they tell me, and that's it. Boom. And then the frequency, you're not like trying to rip their dismember them. You're just offering, they can avail themselves if you're a servant. And that the old hard core, the hard closed sales model is just not very effective these days. Consultative selling is where it's at. All right. The next to the line, oh, intermediary or feedback. Like, what can you put as an intermediary? Can you put someone else talking about it? Can you put a spokesperson in there? Things don't have to be direct. Can you put words into someone else's mouth? A lot of times, something overheard is much more powerful. You know, this from my friend Walter Birch, right? He always talks. He always talks about that if you overhear two people talking about something, that's much more powerful than someone talking to you, trying to convince you. Right. When EF Hutton talks, people listen when they've got all the people in the restaurant turning their heads. Right. And so how can you make it a little less direct, a little more overheard? There's an intermediary there. I heard someone say, you could take a headline that's a direct media you promise and change it into a call from overheard and test that. That's as simple as that. Yeah. But I think that's where the user generated content comes in as well. Instead of just using, oh yeah, like testimonials, they're going, Oh my God, I just used Blissey and I work up on my skin is so smooth and all that kind of dislike. They're saying it. They're saying it and you're not saying it. So you're not boasting, but they're endorsing you. And I guess that's where the influencers use their cloud to, to endorse things rather than just give a blatant testimonial that can be made up anyway. Yeah. That's a great example of an intermediary too is influencer. Hmm. That can be so powerful. Next to last, changing the shape. I always don't remember first names. John McPhee, the famous fiction writer, always talks about writing in terms of make a shape, make a diagram of what you're going to write about, right? Is it a straight line? He was born, then he died. Is it a circle? We start with maybe his death and then we go to his earlier life and then we circle back to when he's dying. Is it a spiral journey of this? Do we go from present to past to future to present to past to future? What is this thing going to look like? And we don't realize it. What we do has shapes, right? Copy has shapes, right? Is it, is it direct into John Ford and Mark Masterson talk about direct or indirect, right? Yes. So it's, do you start from an indirect point and go to a direct point? Do you go around in a circle? If you ever read, what's his name that used to write for boardroom? He, I can't remember his name. Pat Gerard? Pat Gerard, thank you. He had a very meandering shape to his writing. It was like a pitchman. I talk about this and then I tell you this and this and this and this. There was no linearity to it at all. It was just like pitching pitching. That's very interesting. I never noticed that, but it was very effective, right? And a pitchman too. There's no linearity to their pitch. There's no like beginning. It's just almost a sort of randomness to it. Just think about that a little bit, right? Whether there's, what's the shape? Is there balance to it? Is there too much of this? What's the shape of your argument? I think too, a lot of times when you change shape, when you change direction, you keep the prospect off balance. Because when things are predictable, people go to sleep. Yep. And same thing when the design is too orderly and neat, symmetrical, same kind of thing. Yeah. So would the shape also be like what we were talking about before with the Alan Sotanich's podcast, Fissel and then Jeff Walker's, the product launch formula? That's kind of shape changing the shape that people are quite familiar with and the magologs. I mean, they just changed what people were used to seeing and then disrupted them enough that then they didn't think, oh, not another one of these again kind of thing. Yeah, absolutely. You literally change the shape of a piece. Like a lot of these things can actually have a physical manifestation as well as a conceptual kind of manifestation. And I don't think there is any, is it? Right? Is it this? Yes, it's whatever it triggers in you. Yeah, because those mockumentaries, I used to write for the truth about cancer and they use those mockumentaries all the time. And because they present them in the way of an authoritative documentary thing, then people are, although it's a blatant like sales letter, it really was. But it's really is that Trojan horse thing that like it looks like this. So let's bring it in. And then they're going back to a lot of your boxes, they're convincing themselves. And you don't have to do as much work to go up against their cherished beliefs, which I think is a big key to getting people to buy what you're selling. Yeah, thank you. That really helps to mentalize it. The last one is changing the look and look could be colored. The last one already. The last one when I turn up the speed. Yeah. Look can be color, right? Literally color or figuratively color, right? Transparency. And again, literally or figuratively, how transparent are you being? Right? Can I turn up the transparency? Can I just be like, what if I were just as honest as I could be? The odds of this working for you, to be honest with you, it's 50%. But boy, if you're one of the 50%, you're gonna be you're gonna be rich. I read a sales piece the other day or watched a video where it said the average, the results of the average person are zero. The average person won't act on the advice. That's so good. But that's very effective, the whole rich jerk thing in a way it was cartoonish, but in a way it was very transparent. It was like, look, I'm rich, you're not, I'm not like you, I'm not gonna say I'm like you because I'm probably not like you. I know you want to be like me and here's how to do it. And the literalness of transparency. Remember the mailing that they used to come in a styrofoam envelope? Like you could see through it? Not a styrofoam. Yeah, clear plastic. Like a cellophane type thing. Yeah, I mean, that's pretty cool. You can see what's inside it. You don't have to open the envelope to see. Jerry C. Pendanteller used to do the old cup and balls trick, where you have the foam balls and you put them under the cups and you go like this and of course, the person never gets it right because you're doing all kinds of sleight of hand. They did it with clear cups. Yeah, that's brilliant. And this sort of thing could make you think of that, right? You go through the thing and what can we do to make our balls in the cups thing better? Oh, transparency. We can make the cups transparent. Yeah, again, everything's like everything else, right? What works for an engineering problem can work to help you write a song, can help you work to write a joke, can help you create a piece of art, can help you write a come up with a hook for your advertisement, can help you come up with a new product. I just think that transparency thing is so important. Like really letting the reader in on what you're doing. Dan Kennedy is really good at that. He's really in a lot of his promotions. He'll be like, look, I'm going to teach you how to do what I'm doing right now, which is to create a desire and step on your corns and make you want something that you may not be the top thing on your list right now. I was just going to say, not really a question or a comment, but I just wanted to say thank you for your body of work that you put together because recently I had a problem and I put it in my cub takeaways, but I'd love to share it with you here is that so for the last few years I've been wearing hearing aids and then I lost hearing in one ear even more recently. But every time I go into the water, I'd like to swim or to kayak. I love being in the water. So I have to take my hearing aids out. And so basically that renders me pretty much deaf in the water. And that's not cool because there's watercraft and power things around. And I want to hear when someone deals out, sharp, because I'm in Australia. Anyway, so what I was thinking about was, okay, so why hasn't someone made waterproof hearing aids? Okay, so I was looking at the obvious problem. But when I started to look at all the different boxes, I went, the hearing aids are not the problem. What if there was another way to waterproof the hearing aids myself and like maybe a waterproof headband that could still let you know the hearing like the sound come in, but the water out and things like that.
